title: About Annotate
subtitle: How this app works, the tech stack, and how to create modules
description: A self-referencing guide to the Annotate app itself. Covers the architecture, module format, key components, diff highlighting, and how to create new learning modules.

lessons.title: What is Annotate?
lessons.guide:
## What is Annotate?

Annotate is a data-driven learning app for code documentation and guided walkthroughs. It presents content in a three-panel layout:

- **Left sidebar** — lesson navigation
- **Middle panel** — code viewer, diagrams, iframes, or images
- **Right panel** — markdown guide notes explaining the content

### How it works

Annotate is a static React app. It has no backend. All module content is fetched at runtime from the [annotate-modules](https://github.com/al-robertson/annotate-modules) GitHub repo via raw.githubusercontent.com.

```
User opens module → App fetches module.json from GitHub
                  → Renders lessons with code + guide panels
                  → Fetches source files on demand
```

This means new modules can be added without changing or redeploying the app.

### Tech stack

| Technology | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| React 19 | UI framework |
| TypeScript | Type safety |
| Vite 8 | Build tool + dev server |
| Tailwind CSS 4 | Styling |
| react-router-dom | Client-side routing (HashRouter) |
| react-markdown + remark-gfm | Markdown rendering |
| react-syntax-highlighter | Code syntax highlighting (Prism) |
| Mermaid.js | Architecture and ER diagrams |

### Key features

- **Multiple view types** — code, diagrams, iframes, images, full-width markdown
- **Diff highlighting** — green/red line markers for PR walkthroughs
- **Copy to clipboard** — on all code blocks
- **Mobile responsive** — hamburger menu + tab switching
- **Studio** — module editor (in development)
- **PR modules** — auto-generated walkthroughs for pull requests

lessons.guide:
## Architecture

### Data Flow

The app is a static SPA with no backend. Content flows from GitHub:

1. **ModuleLanding** fetches `index.json` to list available modules
2. **ModuleViewer** fetches `{moduleId}/module.json` for lesson definitions
3. **ModuleCodePanel** fetches `{moduleId}/files/{filename}` for source code

All fetches go to `raw.githubusercontent.com` via the configurable `MODULES_BASE_URL`.

### Component Structure

- **main.tsx** — defines routes using HashRouter
- **ModuleLanding** — the home page grid of module cards
- **ModuleViewer** — the three-panel layout that renders lessons
- **Panel components** — each view type has its own panel (code, diagram, iframe, image, markdown)
- **Studio** — the module editor (separate route)

### Why HashRouter?

The app uses `HashRouter` (URLs like `/#/module/my-module`) instead of `BrowserRouter`. This means it works as a static site without server-side routing — any static host (GitHub Pages, S3, nginx) can serve it without rewrite rules.

lessons.title: config.ts
lessons.guide:
## src/config.ts

The single configuration point for where modules are loaded from.

### MODULES_BASE_URL

This controls where the app fetches all module content. It defaults to the `annotate-modules` GitHub repo via raw.githubusercontent.com.

### How to override

Set the `VITE_MODULES_BASE_URL` environment variable:

```bash
# Use local files during development
VITE_MODULES_BASE_URL=/modules npm run dev

# Point at a different repo or CDN
VITE_MODULES_BASE_URL=https://cdn.example.com/modules npm run build
```

### Why this matters

Every component that fetches module data imports `MODULES_BASE_URL` from this file. Changing the source is a single config change — no code modifications needed.

lessons.title: types.ts
lessons.guide:
## src/types.ts

The TypeScript interfaces that define the module data format.

### Key types

- **`Lesson`** — a single lesson within a module. The `view` field determines what appears in the middle panel. Optional fields like `file`, `url`, `diagrams`, `addedLines`, `removedLines` are used by specific view types.

- **`ModuleConfig`** — the full module definition loaded from `module.json`. Contains metadata (title, subtitle, description) and an array of lessons.

- **`ModulesIndex`** — the listing loaded from `index.json`. Contains an array of `ModuleMeta` entries shown on the home page.

### View types

| Type | Middle panel | Required fields |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| `code` | Syntax-highlighted file | `file` |
| `diagram` | Mermaid charts | `diagrams` |
| `iframe` | Embedded URL | `url` |
| `markdown` | Full-width guide | `guide` only |
| `image` | Image display | `url` |

### Diff highlighting fields

`addedLines` and `removedLines` are optional number arrays. When present, the code panel highlights those lines green or red — used for PR walkthrough modules.

lessons.title: ModuleViewer.tsx
lessons.guide:
## src/components/ModuleViewer.tsx

The main component that renders a module's lessons in the three-panel layout.

### What it does

1. Reads the `moduleId` from the URL params
2. Fetches `module.json` from the modules source
3. Renders a sidebar with numbered lesson navigation
4. Renders the active lesson's content in the middle panel
5. Renders the guide markdown in the right panel

### The `renderContentPanel` switch

The key logic is in `renderContentPanel()` — a switch statement that maps the lesson's `view` type to the correct panel component:

- `code` → `ModuleCodePanel` (with diff props)
- `diagram` → `DiagramPanel`
- `iframe` / `swagger` → `IframePanel`
- `image` → `ImagePanel`
- `markdown` → full-width `GuidePanel`

### Responsive design

On desktop: sidebar + content + guide side by side.
On mobile: hamburger menu for sidebar, tabs to switch between content and guide.

### Error handling

If the module can't be loaded, a centered error card is shown with a back button. This handles cases where a module ID in the URL doesn't exist in the modules repo.

lessons.title: ModuleCodePanel.tsx
lessons.guide:
## src/components/ModuleCodePanel.tsx

The code viewer component with syntax highlighting and diff support.

### How it works

1. Fetches the source file from `{MODULES_BASE_URL}/{moduleId}/files/{file}`
2. Detects the language from the file extension
3. Renders with `react-syntax-highlighter` using the Prism oneDark theme
4. Optionally highlights changed lines using `addedLines` / `removedLines`

### Diff highlighting

The `lineProps` callback checks each line number against the `addedSet` and `removedSet`:

- **Added lines** → green left border + subtle green background (12% opacity)
- **Removed lines** → red left border + subtle red background
- **Unchanged lines** → no decoration

The header bar shows `+N lines` / `-N lines` counts when diff data is present.

### Theme customisation

The `cleanTheme` strips background colours from the oneDark theme tokens so the diff highlighting backgrounds show through cleanly.

### Language detection

The `EXT_TO_LANG` map covers common file types. Falls back to `text` for unknown extensions.

lessons.title: ModuleLanding.tsx
lessons.guide:
## src/components/ModuleLanding.tsx

The home page that displays available modules as a card grid.

### How it works

1. Fetches `index.json` from the modules source
2. Renders each module as a clickable card with title, subtitle, and description
3. Clicking a card navigates to `/module/{moduleId}`

### The Studio button

At the bottom of the page, a green button links to the Studio (`/#/studio`) — the module editor.

### Module cards

Each card shows:
- **Title** — bold, turns blue on hover
- **Subtitle** — smaller gray text (e.g. task key + repo name for PR modules)
- **Description** — smallest text, explains what the module covers
- **Arrow icon** — visual affordance that it's clickable

### Error state

If `index.json` can't be loaded (e.g. GitHub is down or the URL is wrong), a centered error card is shown.

lessons.title: Creating Modules
lessons.guide:
## How to Create a Module

### Manual creation

1. Create a folder in the `annotate-modules` repo: `my-module/`
2. Add `my-module/module.json` with the lesson definitions
3. Add source files in `my-module/files/` (for code view lessons)
4. Update `index.json` to include the new module entry
5. Push to main — the module appears in the app immediately

### module.json template

```json
{
  "id": "my-module",
  "title": "My Module Title",
  "subtitle": "Optional subtitle",
  "description": "Shown on the home page card",
  "lessons": [
    {
      "id": "intro",
      "title": "Introduction",
      "view": "markdown",
      "guide": "## Welcome\n\nYour guide content here..."
    },
    {
      "id": "main-code",
      "title": "main.py",
      "view": "code",
      "file": "main.py",
      "guide": "## Main module\n\nExplanation of the code..."
    }
  ]
}
```

### PR modules (automatic)

The Kiro task workflow (step 8) can generate modules automatically for PRs. When Kiro asks "Want a learning module for this PR?" and you say yes, it:

1. Reads the changed files from the PR
2. Generates annotated walkthroughs for each file
3. Pushes the module to `annotate-modules` with diff highlighting data
4. Adds a link to the PR description

PR module IDs follow the format `pr-{repo}-{pr-number}`.

### Using Studio

The Studio (accessible from the green button on the home page) provides a visual editor for creating and editing modules. It's currently in development.

### Tips

- Start with a `markdown` overview lesson to set context
- Use `code` lessons for the main content — code on the left, explanation on the right
- Add `diagram` lessons for architecture overviews
- Keep guide text concise — explain *why*, not just *what*
- For PR modules, include `addedLines` to highlight what changed
